mappings.rationale: Directly covers emotional states including the nature of emotion, the roles of the nervous system, and facial, vocal, and postural expression (section B).
Provides the broad framework for understanding motivational states, emotional states, and transient states affecting behaviour.
Directly covers dreams as transient states affecting behaviour and experience (section C.2).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationale: Directly covers sleep as a transient state affecting behaviour and experience (section C.1).
Anxiety is specifically named as a key human motive requiring special attention (section A.2) and is also an emotional state.
Covers the pleasant dimension of emotional states and hedonic theories of motivation (sections A.1, B).
Sexual behaviour and deviations are specifically named as requiring special attention in motivational states (section A.2).
Covers the neural systems underlying motivation, emotion, and arousal conditions, including the roles of the nervous system in emotion (section B.2).
Covers the neuroscience of motivation, emotional processing, and the brain systems involved in arousal and drive states.
Directly relevant to states of altered consciousness including hypnosis (section C.3) and the subjective aspects of experience.
Psychoanalytic theory is explicitly cited as a diverse theory of motivation (section A.1), covering drives, instincts, and unconscious motivation.
Jungian psychology addresses motivational drives, archetypes, and the relationship between conscious and unconscious states.
Psychoanalytic theories are explicitly named as diverse theories of motivation (section A.1), covering drives and unconscious motivation.
Drugs are listed as a factor affecting time perception and are relevant to altered states of consciousness and fatigue (sections C.3, C.4).
Relevant to motivational states involving needs, desires, drives, and reinforcement, as well as hedonic and incentive theories of motivation.
Covers situational and interactional factors including social stresses, cognitive evaluation, and cognitive dissonance (section A.3).
Covers incentive theories and reinforcement as motivational factors, and cognitive evaluation in decision-making (sections A.1, A.4).
Covers the biological basis of sleep-wake cycles and their effects on arousal, fatigue, and other transient states (sections C.1, C.4).
Covers a fundamental emotional and motivational state relevant to desires, needs, and the affective dimension of behaviour.
Pavlov's work on conditioning is foundational to drive and arousal theories of motivation and reinforcement-based approaches (sections A.1, A.4).
